claims.claim_text: Article 4 of the AI Act requires providers and deployers of AI systems to ensure a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with AI systems on their behalf.
claims.notes: This is safe regulatory context for the category, not proof that prompt engineering alone satisfies the obligation.

claims.claim_text: The AI literacy obligation already applies from 2 February 2025, while supervision and enforcement begin from 3 August 2026.
claims.notes: Use this narrow timing instead of a single August 2026 obligation claim.

claims.claim_text: Luxembourg has a public company-training support route under which the State contributes to employer training plans.
claims.notes: This proves the scheme exists at route level only.

claims.claim_text: Luxembourg company-training aid can cover 15% of annual training investment, and the State contribution rises to 35% of wage costs for certain eligible participants.
claims.notes: Keep this as scheme-level context. Product-level eligibility remains unproven.

claims.claim_text: House of Training publicly offers a Luxembourg prompt-engineering course for financial-sector employees, delivered as a 1h15 e-learning format in French.
claims.notes: Direct evidence that local prompt-engineering supply already exists.

claims.claim_text: House of Training publicly offers a Luxembourg ChatGPT business course whose objectives include designing effective prompts with a solid understanding of prompt engineering and integrating ChatGPT to improve operational efficiency.
claims.notes: This is another direct local substitute for generic prompt-engineering knowledge.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI Academy publishes a free public prompting resource and defines prompt engineering as designing and refining input to help ChatGPT give the best possible answer.
claims.notes: This does not kill the workshop, but it kills any scarcity claim around generic prompting knowledge.

claims.claim_text: Public Luxembourg AI training already links prompting to privacy, bias, and governance concerns.
claims.notes: Safe framing is category context, not a claim that the proposed workshop itself already covers governance sufficiently.

claims.claim_text: House of Training already offers prompt-engineering training in Luxembourg.
claims.notes: The safe wording is already broad enough; the public pages confirm prompt-engineering and prompt-design training exists locally.

claims.claim_text: Free public guidance exists for generic prompt engineering.
Practical prompt use should be combined with privacy and governance awareness.
claims.notes: Safe as category guidance only, not as proof of the product's current curriculum.

claims.claim_text: Luxembourg company training co-funding is relevant to this product category.
claims.notes: The scheme exists, but no public evidence proves the exact workshop qualifies under generic CVT co-funding or any AI-specific package.

claims.claim_text: The workshop will deliver a company-specific prompt library of 30-50 tested prompts.
claims.notes: This is a proposed deliverable, not a publicly verifiable fact.

claims.claim_text: Customization is the main differentiator, not knowledge.
claims.notes: Possible positioning thesis, but not externally verified.

claims.claim_text: SME Package AI 70% applies to this workshop.
claims.notes: No public source in the rerun proves this specific package applies.

claims.claim_text: The workshop can be sold as a 1-2 day format with a prompt library outcome.
claims.notes: The public evidence proves adjacent competitor formats, not this exact product format.
